skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guides Git commit messages, branch names, pull requests, and release workflows using Conventional Commits and GitFlow practices.
skill.summary: Standardizes Git collaboration with commit, branch, pull request, and release workflow guidance.
skill.author: AI-Vibe-Prompts

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Markdown backticks and a weak-crypto pattern, but most hits are false positives from prose, code fences, and placeholder text. The skill does include pre-commit shell commands, including npm scripts, which are legitimate but should be treated as external command execution in untrusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: External Command Checklist Requires User Trust
medium_findings.description: The pre-commit checklist includes shell commands for scanning staged diffs and running npm quality gates. This is normal for a Git workflow skill, but npm scripts can execute project-defined code and should only be run in trusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly presents shell commands in a bash checklist. The intent is legitimate quality control, but npm scripts are a real execution surface.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static findings at the listed locations are Markdown inline code, fenced examples, branch names, or commit message examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not show command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain Markdown formatting and example text. No executable Ruby code, command substitution, or user-controlled shell invocation is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is a Substring Match
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match text inside the description fields, including the word description. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic API use appears in the skill file.
low_findings.static_finding: blocker weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md:3,37
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain prose and naming templates only. I found no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Script Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The checklist recommends npm lint, typecheck, and test scripts. These commands may execute repository-controlled scripts, so users should apply normal trust checks before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the checklist and are common developer actions. The risk depends on the repository scripts, not on malicious skill content.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time to inconsistent commits, branches, and pull request habits. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear Git workflow rules for cleaner collaboration.
actual_capabilities: Defines Conventional Commit message structure and accepted commit types.
Provides branch naming guidance using type, ticket ID, and description.
Explains GitFlow paths for feature, release, hotfix, develop, and main branches.
Lists a pre-commit checklist for secret checks and quality gates.
Auto-invokes for common Git terms such as commit, push, branch, merge, and pull request.
limitations: Does not create branches, commits, or pull requests by itself.
Does not validate repository-specific branch protection rules.
Does not replace human code review or release approval.
Quality gate commands depend on scripts defined by the local project.

use_cases.title: Prepare Consistent Commits
use_cases.description: Convert a set of changes into clear Conventional Commit messages before saving work.

use_cases.title: Guide Team Branch Naming
use_cases.description: Align feature, bugfix, and hotfix branches with ticket identifiers and readable descriptions.

use_cases.title: Structure Release Flow
use_cases.description: Plan how feature, release, and hotfix branches move between develop and main.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message with a short reason.

prompt_templates.title: Name a Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Git branch name for this task using the skill format: [describe the task and ticket ID].

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare a pull request summary, checklist, and review notes for these changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitFlow release or hotfix plan for this repository state and explain each merge target.

output_examples.input: I changed login validation and added tests.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit: fix(auth): validate login input. Reason: the change fixes authentication behavior and includes supporting tests.

output_examples.input: Ticket CORE-456 fixes a null pointer in the API layer.
output_examples.output: Suggested branch: bugfix/CORE-456-fix-null-pointer. This follows the type, ticket, and description pattern.

output_examples.input: We need to ship a hotfix from main and keep develop updated.
output_examples.output: Recommended flow: branch from main, apply the hotfix, merge back to main for release, then merge into develop.

best_practices: Review staged changes before choosing a commit type and scope.
Run project quality gates only after confirming the repository is trusted.
Keep branch names short, searchable, and tied to a ticket when available.
anti_patterns: Using vague commit subjects such as update, changes, or fix stuff.
Running repository scripts from untrusted projects without inspection.
Merging release or hotfix branches without updating both required targets.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and checklists. The user or agent must decide whether to run commands.

faq.question: Which commit format does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Conventional Commits with a type, optional scope, and concise subject.

faq.question: Does it support GitFlow?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes main, develop, feature, release, and hotfix branch paths.

faq.question: Can it help with pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide summaries, review preparation, and collaboration practices.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The checklist includes npm scripts, which can execute repository-defined code.

faq.question: Can teams adapt the naming rules?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams can keep the same structure while changing ticket prefixes or branch types.
